questions.question: The maximum number of edges in a n-node undirected graph without self loops is A. n^2  B. n(n-1)/2  C. n-1  D. (n+1)(n)/2
questions.to_find: Maximum possible number of edges in a simple undirected graph on n vertices

understand.plain: A simple undirected graph has no self loops and at most one edge between any pair of distinct vertices. So you get the most edges by joining every pair of vertices exactly once. The count of such pairs is just 'choose 2 from n', which gives the complete graph K_n.

keywords.explain: A graph with no self loops and no multiple (parallel) edges, so each edge links two distinct vertices.
keywords.example: On 3 vertices the densest simple graph is the triangle K_3 with 3 edges.

keywords.explain: The graph where every pair of distinct vertices is joined by exactly one edge; it achieves the maximum edge count.
An edge {u,v} is the same as {v,u}, so we count pairs with combinations, not permutations.
keywords.example: Vertices a and b give one edge, not two.

understand.visual_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 360 220' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect class='bg' x='2' y='2' width='356' height='216' rx='10'/><text class='lbl ink' x='90' y='30'>Complete graph K_4</text><line class='edge' x1='110' y1='80' x2='250' y2='80' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2.5'/><line class='edge' x1='110' y1='80' x2='110' y2='180' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2.5'/><line class='edge' x1='110' y1='80' x2='250' y2='180' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2.5'/><line class='edge' x1='250' y1='80' x2='110' y2='180' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2.5'/><line class='edge' x1='250' y1='80' x2='250' y2='180' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2.5'/><line class='edge' x1='110' y1='180' x2='250' y2='180' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2.5'/><circle class='hi' cx='110' cy='80' r='12'/><circle class='hi' cx='250' cy='80' r='12'/><circle class='hi' cx='110' cy='180' r='12'/><circle class='hi' cx='250' cy='180' r='12'/><text class='sub mut' x='100' y='208'>every pair joined once: C(4,2) = 6 edges</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Four vertices all joined pairwise (K_4), illustrating that the maximum edge count is the number of vertex pairs.

given.aim: Find the largest number of edges a simple undirected graph on n vertices can have.

terms.meaning: number of vertices (nodes) in the graph
terms.connects: It is the size from which we choose pairs.

terms.meaning: an unordered link {u,v} between two distinct vertices
terms.connects: Each edge corresponds to one chosen vertex pair.

terms.meaning: an edge from a vertex to itself, which is forbidden here
terms.connects: Excluding it means we only count pairs of distinct vertices.

given.plan: Count the number of ways to choose 2 distinct vertices out of n; each such pair can host exactly one edge, so the maximum is C(n,2).

steps.title: Each edge is a pair of distinct vertices
steps.formula_raw: max edges = number of distinct vertex pairs
steps.apply: Without self loops and without multi-edges, every possible edge is determined by an unordered pair {u,v} with u != v.
steps.note: Putting an edge on every pair gives the densest simple graph, namely K_n.

steps.title: Count the pairs with combinations
steps.apply: C(n,2) = n(n-1)/2, so the maximum number of edges is n(n-1)/2.
steps.note: Compare options: n^2 and (n+1)(n)/2 overcount (they allow loops or double counting), n-1 is just a spanning tree.

solution.result: Maximum number of edges = n(n-1)/2, which is option B.

questions.formula_note: The key idea is that a simple graph's edges are exactly the unordered vertex pairs, so the maximum is C(n,2) = n(n-1)/2.

questions.question: Let G be an arbitrary graph with n nodes and k components. If a vertex is removed from G, the number of components in the resultant graph must necessarily lie down between A. k and n  B. k-1 and k+1  C. k-1 and n-1  D. k+1 and n-k
questions.to_find: Tightest range for the number of components after deleting one vertex

understand.plain: Removing one vertex can never split the graph into wildly more pieces than the leftover vertices allow, and it can only reduce the component count by at most one. The lowest possible count happens when you delete an isolated vertex sitting in a component of its own (loses 1 component, giving k-1). The highest happens when one vertex was the hub holding everything together and all n-1 remaining vertices fall apart into singletons (giving n-1).

keywords.explain: A maximal connected piece of the graph; vertices in different components have no path between them.
keywords.example: Two disjoint triangles form a graph with 2 components.

keywords.explain: A vertex whose removal increases the number of components.
keywords.example: The centre of a star graph is a cut vertex; deleting it isolates every leaf.

keywords.explain: A vertex of degree 0 that is its own component.
keywords.example: Deleting it lowers the component count from k to k-1.

understand.visual_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 420 220' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect class='bg' x='2' y='2' width='416' height='216' rx='10'/><text class='lbl ink' x='40' y='30'>Star: hub holds 5 leaves together</text><line class='edge' x1='210' y1='110' x2='110' y2='60' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2.5'/><line class='edge' x1='210' y1='110' x2='110' y2='160' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2.5'/><line class='edge' x1='210' y1='110' x2='310' y2='60' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2.5'/><line class='edge' x1='210' y1='110' x2='310' y2='160' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2.5'/><line class='edge' x1='210' y1='110' x2='210' y2='190' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2.5'/><circle class='hi' cx='110' cy='60' r='11'/><circle class='hi' cx='110' cy='160' r='11'/><circle class='hi' cx='310' cy='60' r='11'/><circle class='hi' cx='310' cy='160' r='11'/><circle class='hi' cx='210' cy='190' r='11'/><circle class='no' cx='210' cy='110' r='13'/><text class='sub mut' x='30' y='210'>delete the hub: 1 component becomes n-1 singletons</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: A star graph whose centre, when deleted, breaks the single component into n-1 isolated vertices, showing the upper bound.

given.aim: Determine the minimum and maximum number of components possible after deleting any single vertex.

terms.meaning: total number of vertices in G
terms.connects: After removing one vertex, n-1 vertices remain, which caps the component count.

terms.meaning: number of connected components of G before deletion
terms.connects: Deletion changes k by losing at most one component or gaining several.

terms.meaning: the single vertex removed from G along with its incident edges
terms.example: v is the hub of a star
terms.connects: Its degree and role decide how the component count shifts.

given.plan: Find the lower bound by deleting an isolated vertex (loses one whole component) and the upper bound by deleting a hub that scatters all other vertices into singletons; bound the result by the remaining vertex count.

steps.title: Lower bound: deleting an isolated vertex
steps.apply: If v is an isolated vertex in its own component, removing it deletes that component entirely and touches no other, so the count drops to k-1. You can never lose more than one component by deleting a single vertex.
steps.note: k-1 is achievable, so it is the true minimum.

steps.title: Upper bound: deleting a hub
steps.apply: If v is adjacent to all other vertices in a single component (like a star centre) and those vertices have no other edges, removing v leaves n-1 isolated vertices, giving n-1 components. With only n-1 vertices left, you can have at most n-1 components.
steps.note: n-1 is achievable, so it is the true maximum.

steps.title: Combine the bounds
steps.apply: Both extremes are attainable, so the number of components after one deletion lies between k-1 and n-1.
steps.note: This matches option C; options A, B and D are either too loose or too tight.

solution.result: The component count lies between k-1 and n-1, which is option C.

questions.formula_note: Deleting one vertex can remove at most one whole component (lower bound k-1) and at most scatters the rest into n-1 singletons (upper bound n-1).

questions.question: Let G_1 = (V, E_1) and G_2 = (V, E_2) be connected graphs on the same vertex set V with more than two vertices. If G_1 intersect G_2 = (V, E_1 intersect E_2) is not a connected graph, then the graph G_1 union G_2 = (V, E_1 union E_2) A. cannot have a cut vertex  B. must have a cycle  C. must have a cut-edge (bridge)  D. has chromatic number strictly greater than those of G_1 and G_2
questions.to_find: A property that the union graph G_1 union G_2 must satisfy

understand.plain: Both G_1 and G_2 are connected on the same n vertices, so each has at least n-1 edges. Their intersection is disconnected, which means they do not share all their edges, so the union strictly contains more edges than a single spanning tree could. Any connected graph on n vertices with more than n-1 edges must contain a cycle, and that is exactly the situation forced here.

keywords.explain: A graph in which there is a path between every pair of vertices.
keywords.example: A path P_n on n vertices is connected with n-1 edges (a tree).

keywords.explain: A connected graph on n vertices with exactly n-1 edges is a tree (no cycle); more than n-1 edges forces a cycle.
keywords.example: Adding any extra edge to a tree creates exactly one cycle.

keywords.explain: G_1 intersect G_2 keeps only common edges; G_1 union G_2 keeps every edge of either graph.
keywords.example: If E_1 and E_2 differ, the union has strictly more edges than the intersection.

understand.visual_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 440 220' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect class='bg' x='2' y='2' width='436' height='216' rx='10'/><text class='lbl ink' x='30' y='28'>Two spanning trees on 4 vertices union to a cycle</text><text class='sub mut' x='60' y='52'>G_1 (path) edges solid, G_2 extra edge dashed</text><line x1='90' y1='90' x2='90' y2='180' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='3'/><line x1='90' y1='90' x2='200' y2='90' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='3'/><line x1='200' y1='90' x2='200' y2='180' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='3'/><line x1='90' y1='180' x2='200' y2='180' stroke='#D85A30' stroke-width='3' stroke-dasharray='7 5'/><circle class='hi' cx='90' cy='90' r='11'/><circle class='hi' cx='200' cy='90' r='11'/><circle class='hi' cx='90' cy='180' r='11'/><circle class='hi' cx='200' cy='180' r='11'/><text class='sub ink' x='250' y='130'>union has 4 edges on</text><text class='sub ink' x='250' y='150'>4 vertices > n-1</text><text class='sub no' x='250' y='172'>=> a cycle is forced</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Two connected graphs on the same vertices overlapping into a 4-cycle, showing that the union exceeds tree edge count and must contain a cycle.

given.aim: Show which property the union of two connected graphs on the same vertex set must have when their intersection is disconnected.

terms.meaning: the common vertex set, with |V| = n > 2
terms.connects: Both graphs and their union live on these same vertices.

terms.meaning: two connected graphs, each with at least n-1 edges
terms.example: two different spanning trees of V
terms.connects: Connectivity forces each to have >= n-1 edges.

terms.meaning: graph on V keeping only the shared edges E_1 intersect E_2
terms.example: the common edges of the two trees
terms.connects: It being disconnected means E_1 != E_2, so the union has > n-1 edges.

given.plan: Use that each connected graph has at least n-1 edges and that the intersection being disconnected forces the union to exceed n-1 edges, which guarantees a cycle.

steps.title: The intersection misses some edges
steps.formula_raw: G_1 intersect G_2 disconnected => E_1 intersect E_2 has fewer than n-1 edges
steps.apply: A connected graph on n vertices needs at least n-1 edges. Since G_1 intersect G_2 is disconnected, its edge set E_1 intersect E_2 has at most n-2 edges, so G_1 and G_2 do not share all of their edges.
steps.note: Hence at least one edge lies in exactly one of the two graphs.

steps.title: The union exceeds tree-edge count
steps.apply: Each connected graph contributes >= n-1 edges, while the shared part has <= n-2 edges, so the union has at least n edges on n vertices.
steps.note: G_1 union G_2 is connected (it contains the connected G_1) and has at least n edges.

steps.title: More than n-1 edges forces a cycle
steps.formula_raw: connected graph on n vertices with > n-1 edges contains a cycle
steps.apply: A connected graph with n or more edges on n vertices is not a tree, so it must contain at least one cycle.
steps.note: Thus G_1 union G_2 must have a cycle, which is option B.

solution.result: G_1 union G_2 must contain a cycle, which is option B.

questions.formula_note: Two connected graphs (each >= n-1 edges) whose intersection is disconnected (<= n-2 shared edges) union to a connected graph with >= n edges, and a connected graph on n vertices with more than n-1 edges always has a cycle.

questions.question: Let G be a simple graph with 20 vertices and 100 edges. The size of the minimum vertex cover of G is 8. Then, the size of the maximum independent set of G is: A. 12  B. 8  C. less than 8  D. more than 12
questions.to_find: Size of the maximum independent set of G

understand.plain: A vertex cover and an independent set are complements of each other: a set of vertices is a vertex cover exactly when the remaining vertices form an independent set. So the largest independent set is just all the vertices left over after removing a smallest vertex cover. With 20 vertices and a minimum vertex cover of size 8, that leftover set has 20 - 8 = 12 vertices.

keywords.explain: A set of vertices that touches (covers) at least one endpoint of every edge.
keywords.example: In a triangle, any 2 of the 3 vertices form a vertex cover.

keywords.explain: A set of vertices with no edge between any two of them.
keywords.example: The two non-adjacent corners of a path form an independent set.

keywords.explain: The complement of a minimum vertex cover is a maximum independent set, so their sizes add up to the vertex count.
keywords.example: On 20 vertices, cover 8 means independent set 12.

understand.visual_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 420 210' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect class='bg' x='2' y='2' width='416' height='206' rx='10'/><text class='lbl ink' x='100' y='32'>20 vertices split in two</text><rect class='no' x='40' y='70' width='150' height='80' rx='8' opacity='0.85'/><rect class='ok' x='230' y='70' width='150' height='80' rx='8' opacity='0.85'/><text class='lbl ink' x='75' y='105'>cover</text><text class='big ink' x='100' y='135'>8</text><text class='lbl ink' x='248' y='105'>independent</text><text class='big ink' x='295' y='135'>12</text><text class='sub mut' x='120' y='185'>8 + 12 = 20 = |V|</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: The 20 vertices split into a minimum vertex cover of 8 and its complementary maximum independent set of 12.

given.aim: Compute the maximum independent set size from the vertex count and minimum vertex cover size.

terms.meaning: number of vertices in the simple graph G
terms.connects: It is the total that the cover and independent set partition.

terms.meaning: smallest set of vertices covering every edge
terms.example: 8 vertices suffice to touch all 100 edges
terms.connects: Its complement is the maximum independent set.

terms.meaning: largest set of mutually non-adjacent vertices, the quantity asked for
terms.connects: Equals |V| minus the minimum vertex cover.

given.plan: Apply Gallai's identity: max independent set = total vertices - minimum vertex cover = 20 - 8.

steps.title: Cover and independent set are complements
steps.formula_raw: S is a vertex cover  <=>  V minus S is an independent set
steps.apply: Removing a vertex cover leaves no edge uncovered, so the remaining vertices have no edge between them, i.e. they are independent. Making the cover smallest makes this remaining independent set largest.
steps.note: This is why minimum cover and maximum independent set are exact complements.

steps.title: Apply the Gallai count
steps.apply: With |V| = 20 and minimum vertex cover tau(G) = 8, the maximum independent set is alpha(G) = 20 - 8 = 12. The 100 edges are extra information not needed for the count.
steps.note: The edge total is a distractor here.

solution.result: Maximum independent set size = 20 - 8 = 12, which is option A.

questions.formula_note: Gallai's identity alpha(G) + tau(G) = |V| gives 20 - 8 = 12 directly; the edge count is a distractor.

questions.question: The 2^n vertices of a graph G corresponds to all subsets of a set of size n, for n >= 6. Two vertices of G are adjacent if and only if the corresponding sets intersect in exactly two elements. The number of connected components in G is: A. n  B. n+2  C. 2^(n/2)  D. 2^n/n
questions.to_find: Number of connected components of the subset-intersection graph G

understand.plain: Every subset of the n-element set is a vertex, and two subsets are joined only when they share exactly two elements. A subset with fewer than two elements (the empty set or any single element) can never share two elements with anyone, so each such subset is an isolated vertex on its own. All subsets of size at least two turn out to be linked into one big connected piece. Counting them up gives the empty set, the n singletons, and one large component.

keywords.explain: A maximal set of vertices reachable from one another via edges.
keywords.example: An isolated vertex is a component of size 1.

keywords.explain: A vertex with no edges; here, any subset that cannot intersect another in exactly two elements.
keywords.example: The empty set shares 0 elements with everyone, so it is isolated.

keywords.explain: Sets with at least two elements that can be linked by repeatedly swapping elements while keeping a 2-element overlap.
keywords.example: {1,2,3} and {2,3,4} share {2,3}, so they are adjacent.

understand.visual_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 440 230' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect class='bg' x='2' y='2' width='436' height='226' rx='10'/><text class='lbl ink' x='90' y='28'>Components of G</text><circle class='no' cx='60' cy='80' r='13'/><text class='sub ink' x='42' y='112'>empty set</text><circle class='no' cx='150' cy='70' r='11'/><circle class='no' cx='190' cy='70' r='11'/><circle class='no' cx='230' cy='70' r='11'/><text class='sub ink' x='150' y='112'>n singletons</text><ellipse class='hi' cx='340' cy='140' rx='80' ry='55'/><text class='sub ink' x='292' y='135'>all sets of</text><text class='sub ink' x='292' y='155'>size >= 2</text><text class='sub mut' x='80' y='215'>1 (empty) + n (singletons) + 1 (big) = n+2 components</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: The empty set and n singleton vertices are isolated components, while all subsets of size at least two form one large component, giving n+2 total.

given.aim: Count the connected components of the graph whose vertices are all subsets of an n-set, with edges for exactly-two-element overlaps.

terms.meaning: size of the ground set, with n >= 6
terms.connects: It fixes how many singleton vertices there are.

terms.meaning: each of the 2^n subsets of the ground set is one vertex
terms.connects: Its size decides whether it can have any edge.

terms.meaning: two subsets are joined iff they intersect in exactly two elements
terms.connects: Only subsets of size >= 2 can satisfy this, so smaller subsets are isolated.

given.plan: Identify which subsets can have no edge (sizes 0 and 1) as isolated components, then argue all subsets of size >= 2 link into a single component, and add up the components.

steps.title: Subsets of size 0 or 1 are isolated
steps.apply: A set with fewer than two elements can intersect any other set in at most |A| < 2 elements, so it never reaches an overlap of exactly two. The empty set and each of the n singletons are therefore isolated vertices.
steps.note: That accounts for 1 + n isolated components.

steps.title: All subsets of size >= 2 form one component
steps.formula_raw: any two sets of size >= 2 are connected by a path of exactly-2-overlap steps
steps.apply: Given any two subsets each of size at least two, you can walk from one to the other by changing one element at a time through intermediate sets that always share exactly two elements (using n >= 6 to keep enough room), so they all lie in a single connected component.
steps.note: This big component contains every subset of size 2 or more.

solution.result: The number of connected components is n + 2, which is option B.

questions.formula_note: Subsets of size 0 and 1 cannot meet the exactly-two-element overlap rule, giving 1 + n isolated vertices, while all subsets of size >= 2 form a single component, totalling n + 2.

questions.question: Which of the following graphs has an Eulerian circuit? A. Any k-regular graph where k is an even number.  B. A complete graph on 90 vertices.  C. The complement of a cycle on 25 vertices.  D. None of the above
questions.answer: C. The complement of a cycle on 25 vertices
questions.to_find: Which graph is guaranteed to have an Eulerian circuit

understand.plain: A connected graph has an Eulerian circuit (a closed walk using every edge exactly once) precisely when every vertex has even degree. Check each option: an even-degree regular graph could still be disconnected, so it is not guaranteed; the complete graph K_90 has every vertex of odd degree 89, so it fails; but the complement of a 25-cycle is connected and every vertex has even degree 22, so it works.

keywords.explain: A closed walk that traverses every edge of the graph exactly once and returns to the start.
keywords.example: A single cycle is itself an Eulerian circuit.

keywords.explain: A connected graph has an Eulerian circuit if and only if every vertex has even degree.
keywords.example: Each vertex of a triangle has degree 2 (even), so the triangle is Eulerian.

keywords.explain: The graph on the same vertices where two vertices are joined exactly when they were not joined originally.
keywords.example: In the complement of C_n each vertex has degree (n-1) - 2 = n-3.

understand.visual_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 440 220' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect class='bg' x='2' y='2' width='436' height='216' rx='10'/><text class='lbl ink' x='40' y='28'>Degree check for an Eulerian circuit</text><text class='no lbl' x='40' y='70'>K_90:</text><text class='sub ink' x='110' y='70'>degree 89 (odd) => no circuit</text><text class='no lbl' x='40' y='110'>k-regular, k even:</text><text class='sub ink' x='220' y='110'>may be disconnected</text><text class='ok lbl' x='40' y='155'>complement of C_25:</text><text class='sub ink' x='250' y='155'>degree 22 (even)</text><text class='ok sub' x='40' y='185'>connected + all even => Eulerian circuit exists</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Degree comparison showing K_90 has odd degree, an even k-regular graph may be disconnected, while the complement of C_25 has even degree 22 and is connected, so only it is Eulerian.

given.aim: Identify which listed graph is guaranteed to have an Eulerian circuit.

terms.meaning: connected and every vertex of even degree
terms.example: all degrees even and one piece
terms.connects: It is the exact test applied to each option.

terms.meaning: complete graph on 90 vertices, each of degree 89
terms.connects: Odd degrees rule out an Eulerian circuit for option B.

terms.meaning: graph on 25 vertices where each had degree 2 in the cycle, so degree 25-1-2 = 22 in the complement
terms.connects: Even degree plus connectivity makes option C Eulerian.

given.plan: Apply the even-degree connectivity test to each option and keep only the graph that satisfies it for certain.

steps.title: Rule out options A and B
steps.formula_raw: Eulerian circuit  <=>  connected and every degree even
steps.apply: Option A: a k-regular graph with k even has all even degrees but can be disconnected (two disjoint even-regular graphs), so it is not guaranteed. Option B: K_90 has every vertex of degree 89, which is odd, so it has no Eulerian circuit.
steps.note: Even degrees alone are not enough; connectivity is also required.

steps.title: Check option C: complement of C_25
steps.formula_raw: deg in complement = (n-1) - deg in C_n = (25-1) - 2 = 22
steps.apply: In the cycle C_25 every vertex has degree 2, so in the complement every vertex has degree 24 - 2 = 22, which is even. The complement of a cycle on n >= 5 vertices is also connected.
steps.note: Connected and all degrees even, so an Eulerian circuit exists.

steps.title: Select the guaranteed graph
steps.apply: Only the complement of C_25 satisfies both conditions for certain, so it is the graph with an Eulerian circuit.

solution.result: The complement of a cycle on 25 vertices has an Eulerian circuit, which is option C.

questions.formula_note: An Eulerian circuit needs a connected graph with all even degrees; K_90 (degree 89, odd) and a possibly disconnected even-regular graph fail, but the complement of C_25 is connected with even degree 22.

formulas.name: Components After Removing One Vertex
formulas.whenToUse: When asked how the number of connected components can change after deleting a single vertex from a graph with n vertices and k components.

terms.means: number of connected components of G before deletion
number of components after removing vertex v

formulas.trap: Removing one vertex lowers the component count by at most one (delete an isolated vertex), but can raise it up to n-1 (delete a hub); do not assume it always stays near k.

formulas.name: Edge Count of a Graph Union (Inclusion-Exclusion on Edges)
formulas.whenToUse: When combining two graphs on the same vertex set and you need the edge count of their union, especially to compare against the tree bound n-1.

terms.means: edge sets of the two graphs on the same vertex set
edges present in at least one graph

formulas.trap: Forgetting to subtract the shared edges double-counts them; the union can have far more edges than either graph alone.

formulas.name: Tree Edge Count and Cycle Condition
formulas.plain: A connected graph on n vertices is a tree iff it has exactly n-1 edges; more than n-1 edges forces a cycle
formulas.whenToUse: When deciding whether a connected graph must contain a cycle based on its edge count relative to n-1.
formulas.trap: Exactly n-1 edges gives a tree (no cycle); the cycle is only forced when edges strictly exceed n-1 in a connected graph.

formulas.name: Gallai Identity (Vertex Cover and Independent Set)
formulas.plain: alpha(G) + tau(G) = |V|  (max independent set + min vertex cover = number of vertices)
formulas.whenToUse: When converting between the size of a minimum vertex cover and the size of a maximum independent set in any graph.

terms.means: size of the maximum independent set
size of the minimum vertex cover

formulas.trap: The complement of a vertex cover is an independent set, so the edge count is irrelevant to this identity; do not be distracted by the number of edges.

formulas.name: Counting Connected Components by Grouping Vertices
formulas.latex: c(G) = (\text{isolated vertices}) + (\text{number of larger connected pieces})
formulas.plain: components(G) = number of isolated vertices + number of larger connected pieces
formulas.whenToUse: When counting connected components by separating vertices that can have no edge from those that link together into bigger pieces.

terms.means: a vertex of degree 0, forming its own component

formulas.trap: Each vertex that cannot satisfy the adjacency rule is its own component; remember to add these isolated vertices to the larger components.

formulas.name: Eulerian Circuit Condition
formulas.latex: \text{Eulerian circuit} \iff G \text{ connected and } \deg(v) \text{ even for all } v
formulas.plain: A connected graph has an Eulerian circuit iff every vertex has even degree
formulas.whenToUse: When deciding whether a graph admits a closed walk that uses every edge exactly once.

terms.means: every pair of vertices (with edges) is joined by a path

formulas.trap: Even degree alone is not sufficient; the graph must also be connected (ignoring isolated vertices), so an even-regular but disconnected graph fails.

formulas.name: Vertex Degree in the Complement Graph
formulas.plain: deg in complement = (n-1) - deg in original
formulas.whenToUse: When computing vertex degrees in the complement of a graph, for example the complement of a cycle.

terms.means: degree of v in the original graph
degree of v in the complement graph

formulas.trap: The maximum possible degree is n-1, so subtract the original degree from n-1, not from n.
